items.prompt_md: Why does the algorithm push **every** rotten orange into the queue before the `while` loop starts, instead of running BFS from one rotten orange at a time?
options: So the queue processes all oranges that rot at the same minute together, keeping the minute count correct
Because `deque` requires every item to be added before any are removed
So the grid can be mutated in place without a visited set
items.answer: So the queue processes all oranges that rot at the same minute together, keeping the minute count correct
items.explanation_md: Seeding all sources at once means each queue "wave" is exactly the set of oranges that rot simultaneously, so incrementing `minutes` once per wave gives the true minimum spread time.

items.prompt_md: You must find the minimum number of rounds for a condition to spread across a grid from several starting cells simultaneously to every reachable cell. Which approach fits?
options: Seed a queue with every starting cell, then BFS level by level, counting one round per queue wave
DFS flood fill from each starting cell separately and take the minimum depth reached
Sort the cells by distance to the nearest starting cell
Binary search on the number of rounds
items.answer: Seed a queue with every starting cell, then BFS level by level, counting one round per queue wave
items.explanation_md: Multiple simultaneous sources plus a "minimum rounds to spread" question on an unweighted grid is the multi-source BFS fingerprint: seed all sources first, then let queue waves count the rounds.

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of the multi-source BFS rotting-oranges solution on an R × C grid?
items.explanation_md: Every cell is enqueued and dequeued at most once across the whole run, so the total work is proportional to the number of cells — O(R × C).
